X6.1.label: Question X6.1
X6.1.topic: Without-profits critical illness policy: investigations and actions
X6.1.synopsis: A large multi-line insurer has mainly sold without-profits business in the past. It has recently written a regular premium without-profits critical illness policy through a direct sales force. Investigations into ongoing loss on this contract and possible actions.
X6.1.scenario: A large multi-line insurer has mainly sold without-profits business in the past. It has recently written a regular premium without-profits critical illness policy through a direct sales force. Initial sales were encouraging, but recently sales have fallen and most of the contracts issued to date have lapsed or been surrendered. The company's recent investigations indicate an ongoing loss on this contract.

X6.1.question: Outline the investigations the insurer should carry out to determine the causes of the current problems and discuss the action it might then take. [16]

Investigations: Overall expected losses may be due to heavy claims (eg from initial poor selection or possibly an epidemic), so should investigate overall actual compared to expected claims on this portfolio. [½]
Need to sub-divide actual vs expected analysis between frequency of claims and the size of the claim (if depending on severity). [½]
Since this is critical illness business, it also has a mortality risk attached. Therefore need to separate out these elements to compare actual and expected for each. [½]
Would need to allow for the reduced exposure for deaths if critical illness claims have been higher than expected. [½]
Would also wish to split data by distribution channel, term, age, gender, region, smoker status, etc so as to see if the problem is arising in particular subsets of the business. [½]
There are significant volumes and so such a split may be possible, but need to ensure credibility of data. [½]
If data permit, also analyse by significant critical illness causes or treatments. [½]
Investigate any broad trends over time, if data allow, for example, whether the problem has been worsening over time. [½]
Any changes to underwriting approach would be relevant. [½]
Population / reinsurers / industry data could be useful for identifying trends. [½]
Investigate the appropriateness of current underwriting levels. [½]
In particular, the balance between the cost of obtaining medical evidence and the resulting level of mortality / critical illness experienced would need to be investigated. [½]
If data permit, investigate whether there is any evidence of early claims, misrepresentation or anti-selection. [½]

Information gained externally on economic conditions (company, general and regional), and other trends (such as the effect of medical advances) will be useful in assessing the effect of these influences. [½]
Expenses: An analysis of expenses will be required. This will split expenses into initial, renewal and termination, ... and into per policy, per premium and per sum assured. [1]
Subjective judgement will be required in the allocation of overheads, such as property costs and some salary costs. [½]
Time sheet analysis may also be needed to split some costs between product types. [½]
Again would want to split between distribution channels. [½]
Also consider how business volumes and average policy size compare with that expected in the pricing. [½]
Withdrawals: This should be analysed by duration in force and distribution channel. [½]
It may even be necessary to analyse by individual financial adviser and salesperson. (The company should be doing this anyway.) [½]
Account should be taken of any past factors which may not apply in the immediate future, eg an economic recession. [½]
Tax: The company should be aware of past tax changes and its current tax position and so this should not need further investigation. [½]
Reinsurance: Should reassess reinsurance requirements, eg by modelling the effect of various reinsurance arrangements, ... to assess the extent and level of reinsurance required and with whom. [1]
Competition: Company should investigate (to the extent it can) how its premium rates, underwriting procedures and definitions of illness compare with its competitors. [½]
It should aim to establish whether its losses are common throughout this market or a company-specific problem. [½]
Further profit-testing: Based on its investigations of past experience, the company can formulate assumptions for the future to enable it to assess whether the losses are likely to continue. [½]
Past experience will have to be adjusted where appropriate, for example to allow for future expense inflation. [½]
The company will also need assumptions about future volumes and mix of business, in particular by distribution channel. [½]

The company can then project forward its expected cashflows on the contract, allowing for the impact on available profit of reserves and required solvency capital, and discount at a suitable risk discount rate to give expected profit. [1]
It should sensitivity test key parameters such as early lapses, mortality / morbidity and business volumes. [½]
It can thus investigate the profit implication of leaving premium rates unchanged, and also determine the premium necessary to restore profitability. [½]
In doing so, however, it must consider the possible impact on volumes if premiums are increased. [½]
Model office: The cashflows from the profit test can be incorporated into a model office to enable the implications for the company as a whole to be fully investigated. [½]
In particular, the company can investigate the effect on: total profit, embedded value, return on capital, free assets and available capital. [1]
Possible responses to the problems: Underwriting procedures: Increase the level of underwriting. [½]
For example, reduce medical limits so that doctors' reports and medical examinations are obtained at lower sums assured. [½]
It is likely that the increased costs will be more than paid for by improved experience. [½]
Must also investigate critical illness claims appropriately. [½]
Expense control: Look carefully at the business processes to ensure that these are as efficient as possible, and not at a disadvantage compared with competitors. [½]
Sell more business to reduce per-policy expenses. [½]
Consider setting a higher minimum policy size, although it is generally worthwhile writing contracts, providing they cover marginal expenses. [½]
Definitions of critical illness: We should consider changing the CI definitions - either the stringency of the criteria of claiming or the number of conditions covered could be changed. [1]
A narrower range of illnesses (or more stringent criteria for claiming) might improve claims experience, but may be unpopular, leading to fewer sales. [½]
In any case, most claims result from a small number of conditions that we would have to cover to remain in the market. [½]
Increasing the range of illnesses covered where this cover is perceived as valuable might improve sales and hence reduce per-policy expenses, but would involve negligible expected claims. [½]
The definitions of illnesses must be very precise to prevent disputes and the need to pay claims for relatively minor illnesses where not intended. [½]
There may be competitive or regulatory constraints on the definitions that can be used, eg ABI guidance. [½]
Withdrawals: Encourage the salesforce to sell business with good persistency. [½]
May need to retrain, or not use, salespeople (or financial advisers) whose lapse experience is unacceptably high. [½]
Set remuneration scales based on high persistency. [½]
Monitor our lapse experience in more detail, ... possibly down to individual seller level. [1]
Review of reinsurance arrangements: The reinsurance arrangements should be reviewed. [½]
This on its own will not stop the business being unprofitable (because the reinsurer is unlikely to accept business on unprofitable terms). [½]
However, it will be useful to examine exactly what the objectives are in reinsuring this business ... and whether or not reinsuring is the most cost-efficient way of achieving these objectives. [1]
Ensure that the reinsurance terms remain competitive. [½]
Changing the premium: Consider changing the premium to bring it in line with the company's current view of the future. [½]
This needs to be considered carefully with the likely effect on the volume of new business. [½]
An increase in the volume of new business will directly increase overall profits and also increase the number of policies over which fixed expenses are spread. [½]
As this is a relatively simple contract, business volumes tend to be fairly sensitive to premium rates. This is particularly true of sales through financial advisers. [½]
Thus, an increase in the premium rate will not necessarily solve the problem because of the consequent reduction in business volumes. [½]
Alternatively, reduce the premium rate because the consequent increase in new business may significantly increase the contribution to profit. [½]
Could charge different premiums through different distribution channels to reflect more accurately their experience, ... eg charge a higher premium for business sold through the DSF. [1]
Consider selling more business with reviewable, rather than guaranteed, premiums and terms to enable premiums and benefits to be changed in the light of experience. [½]
This may be more in line with the competition but may not be popular with financial advisers. [½]
Withdrawing from the market: Could withdraw from the market completely. [½]
Quantify the savings resulting from withdrawing and assess these against the possible adverse effects. [½]
These include: loss of goodwill from financial advisers, a reduction in the range of products, which would not be ideal for our DSF, lower (or no) contribution made towards the fixed expenses. [1½]
Given that this is a significant class of business for the company, only consider this if it is impossible to correct the current problems. [½]
Accept as a loss leader: Could continue to write this business at a loss. [½]
This would be to avoid the problems associated with withdrawing from the market completely. [½]

However, would not recommend this approach because: current losses are too big to accept as a loss leader, product may unwittingly become too competitive and company will start attracting large volumes of unprofitable business. [1]

X6.2.label: Question X6.2
X6.2.topic: Reasons for poor performance in PMI and actuary's suggestions
X6.2.synopsis: A life insurance company sells most of its regular premium private medical insurance (PMI) business through IFAs and a direct sales force. Analysis of problems. Evaluation of offering a loyalty discount and selling through the internet.
X6.2.scenario: A life insurance company sells most of its regular premium private medical insurance (PMI) business through a network of independent financial advisers (IFAs) and through a direct sales force. Over the last two years, the underlying level of overall technical provisions of this business class has increased substantially above the levels planned, despite increases in premium rates. It appears that the volumes of new business, and persistency on existing policies, are lower than expected.

(i).question: Discuss the reasons why the business might be experiencing these particular problems. [12]

(a) Low volumes of new business: Competition: Low volumes of in-force business will be due to low new business sales and/or poor persistency. This could be due to: increased competition in the PMI market, competitors selling similar products more cheaply. [1]
There are several reasons why some competitors might be able to do this:
They may be prepared to accept smaller profits (even losses) initially in order to build up volumes and market awareness. [½]
This will remain for as long as insurers are prepared to accept such profits / losses. Some of these competitors may therefore leave the market relatively soon. [½]
These new entrants might take advantage of the fact that claims are relatively low in the first few years of a policy ('initial selection'). [½]

This arises because pre-existing conditions are normally excluded (either specifically or as a general condition). Over time, new medical conditions will arise on these policies, which will be covered, and so there will be more claims. [½]
Currently new entrants will have a large proportion of new policyholders, and so premiums could be kept low, ... but in time these will need to be increased as the business matures. [1]

They may have a different rating structure or use rating factors not used by the insurer (such as occupation or location), and so attract the type of customers for whom they can offer a lower premium. [½]
If their premium structures accurately reflect the eventual claims the competitors should still make profits (otherwise they may make losses). [½]

In particular, they may mainly target young people. As premiums for younger ages have traditionally been higher than necessary, such insurers may be able to offer cheaper rates (because there are no old people requiring cross subsidies). [½]
This is sustainable for as long as mainly young people are on the insurer's books, but the existing customers will age with time. [½]
They may have inaccurate premiums, particularly if they have very little data for pricing. [½]
This will be corrected over time, either because more data will reveal that prices need to be increased or losses will force the competitor out of the market. [½]
They may be willing to take on new customers with lower underwriting standards, perhaps on the same terms as their previous insurer. [½]
This is likely to result in more claims as the experience develops. [½]
They may be using stricter underwriting to attract better risks, but at a cheaper premium. This is sustainable if the overall saving exceeds the cost of additional underwriting. [1]
They may be more efficient, eg taking advantage of new technology, and so have low selling and administration costs. This may be sustainable in the long run. [½]

They may have a more attractive product, either because of better or innovative product design (eg they may offer additional services to corporate clients), ... or because of the way it has been presented (eg lots of advertising or aggressive marketing). [1]
The sales method used by competitors may have been very successful, eg remuneration of sales staff encourages and motivates very effectively. [½]
Other reasons for poor sales and persistency: The main deterrent to renewing is likely to be the size of premium increases on renewal. [½]
The overall annual increase is often well in excess of price inflation and so can be more than many policyholders can afford. [½]

This is because: to maintain profit levels, premiums need to keep pace with increasing claims (see below), each individual's premium will (usually) need to increase to allow for their increasing age. [1]
Non-claimers may not wish to renew because they feel that they have not had any benefit from the product. [½]
Policyholders may leave if they have had poor customer service from the insurer - eg if they feel that their claim has been dealt with insensitively. [½]
Some badly-handled cases may have resulted in bad publicity for the insurer resulting in other policyholders wishing to cancel. [½]
There may be generally less demand for PMI. This could be as a result of improvements to the State healthcare services or bad publicity about PMI in general, which could affect sales. [1]
An economic downturn could result in people having less disposable income to purchase a low-priority product, such as PMI. [½]
(b) Low profitability: Claims experience: The falling profits could be due to rising claim rates. [½]

If the insurer's rating structure does not truly reflect the risk, the mix of business may have been more towards sectors where the increase in claims has been greater than the corresponding increase in premiums (ie anti-selection). [½]
For example, there may be an increase in the average age of people covered where premiums do not increase with age by as much as claims do. [½]

The number of claims per policy could be increasing due to: a genuine increase in number of people requiring treatment, greater demand for medical treatment as people's expectations increase, increasing numbers of people becoming aware that they have PMI cover, the increasing maturity of policyholders (ie the initial selection effect mentioned above) - but this will be more of a problem for insurers new to PMI. [2]

The amount of claims could be increasing due to: increasing medical inflation, which is usually significantly higher than general inflation, ... eg due to the increasing costs of medical equipment or consultants' fees. [1]

the impact of improving medical technology. The type of treatment used for a particular illness could change and be more expensive. In addition, there are many diseases for which there are now treatments, which were not available a few years ago. [1]

The standards of medical underwriting could be poor due to, for example: insufficient medical evidence being collected, poor training of underwriting staff, lowering standards as a result of cost cutting. [1½]
These may result in claims for pre-existing conditions being (wrongly) paid out. [½]
There may have been poor claims control, eg due to cutbacks or poor training. [½]
This may result in treatments being covered that should not have been, or in a higher amount of claims being paid out. [½]
Premiums: The insurer's premium rates may be lower than required to make sufficient profit due to competitive (and possibly moral) pressure to keep premium increases lower than claims inflation. [½]

In addition, overall premiums levels may be less than assumed (despite premium increases) due to changes in the mix of business, for example: people purchasing (or switching to) less valuable products, eg with higher excesses, fewer people being covered by each policy, eg as a result of reducing family sizes. [1]
All of these can have adverse effects on overall profitability. [½]
The premium rates may also be insufficient cover the technical provisions, leading to a reduction in profits. [½]
Expenses: Expenses may have increased by more than expected, eg due to increased claims administration costs as conditions become more complicated. [½]
However, expense reductions may reduce the quality of customer service, and so more may need to be spent in order to improve this. [½]
(c) Technical provisions: The increase in technical provision could be as a result of either the best estimate liability increasing or the risk margin increasing. [½]

The best estimate liability may have increased due to a change to the: model points used to calculate an approximate best estimate liability, assumptions used (these should always be best estimate), discount rate used. [1½]

The assumptions could need updating due to: large claims becoming longer tailed, eg if treatments become more complex, some may take longer to settle, a backlog in the payment of claims (particularly large ones), which has been getting worse. [1]

The risk margin may have increased due to a change to the: capital required for each future projected year, cost of capital rate required to be used, discount rate used, method for approximating the risk margin (eg changing the driver used), change to any diversification benefit. [2½]
The calculated provisions may have been incorrect, eg due to: an inappropriate method, incorrect data being used. [1]

(ii).question:
In order to deal with the problems identified, a newly qualified actuary has suggested that a 'loyalty discount' scale is offered to personal customers, under which the size of the discount reflects the customer's duration with the company since the purchase of the policy. The actuary has further suggested that new customers are obtained by switching the emphasis largely towards sales through the internet rather than mainly through IFAs.

Discuss the actuary's suggestions and describe briefly what other actions the company should carry out. [15]

Discussion of ideas with suggested impro: Loyalty discount to personal customers: This would apply a discount to the customers' premium on subsequent renewal. The size of the discount would reflect the time that the customer has had a PMI policy with the insurer. [½]

Possible advantages are: it encourages the customer to remain with the original insurer, and so will keep in-force business volumes high, can be justified on the basis that the cost of acquiring business is greater than the cost of retaining a policy at renewal, it is a new idea, so it might attract customers who would not otherwise purchase PMI. [1½]

Possible disadvantages are: if premiums do not fully reflect the effect of increasing age on claims (ie younger customers are cross subsiding older ones), per-policy profits from older customers will be lower, claims are expected to increase as the initial selection effect (described above) wears off, the perceived impact of the discount on an individual's premium may be absorbed by the effect of inflationary premium increases, and so may not be appreciated by customers. [1½]
The expected effect of the discount should be allowed for when setting the initial premium. [½]
New customers may need to be charged a higher premium if the cost of the discount cannot be offset by the increase in profits gained from retaining more customers. [½]
A claims-related discount could be applied, eg based on the number of claim-free years. [½]
Advantages of a claims-related discount are: This would encourage customers to renew, but in particular those customers with good claims experience. [½]
This is a proxy to state of health and is a good indication of the likelihood of future claims. [½]
It may also discourage people from making small claims, which reduces administration costs. [½]
Disadvantages of a claims-related discount are: Again, the initial premium may need to be higher than previously, but an introductory discount for new customers may also be considered. [½]
Can be difficult to decide what constitutes a 'claim'. [½]

For example, one customer who claims for a series of treatments for a malignant tumour will have a very different state of health to someone who has just had an initial consultation for a suspect lump for which the result is 'all clear'. [½]
There are ethical issues of whether customers should be penalised for being ill - something that is largely out of their control. [½]
The insurer could also be accused of discouraging customers from taking medical advice or treatment. [½]

The mechanism for assessing the discount will need to be developed. It should be simple enough for customers to understand it, but not so simple as to prevent the desired financial effect being achieved. [½]
Administrative and pricing systems will have to be adapted to cope with the discount. [½]
Also consider: performing modelling to assess the financial effect of the chosen scale, conducting market research to assess whether it will be fair and acceptable. [1]

Selling through the internet: The main advantages are: Using a different distribution channel may increase sales. Many of the new competitors may be selling much of their business on the internet. [½]
It may appeal to a different market segment. For example, PMI traditionally appeals to well-off middle-aged customers, so this might attract younger customers (who on average claim less). [½]
Sales may be quicker, reducing selling costs, as there is much less human involvement. [½]
If the main company already have an established internet operation, this could be adapted and used quickly and cheaply. [½]
The main disadvantages are: Less information is likely to be collected at the point of purchase because the delay in the sales process due to underwriting assessments may not be acceptable. [½]
Less information will make accurate medical underwriting more difficult. [½]
The moratorium method of excluding pre-existing conditions would therefore be more likely. [½]
There is a greater risk of the customer not understanding exactly what has been purchased (eg exclusions, limits and moratorium). [½]
A simpler product design may need to be developed to reduce the risk of complaints from aggrieved claimants later. [½]
There is a greater risk of anti-selection or of customers lying about their personal details in order to get a cheaper price. [½]
The insurer should ensure that their rating structures are at least as good as their competitors, and take steps to check the validity of information given. [½]
It may need to allow for the set-up costs of establishing an internet selling operation. [½]
Again, financial modelling should be carried out to assess the financial impact. [½]
Other actions: Competition: Investigate the reasons why competitors are able to sell similar PMI products more cheaply, and their methods of selling, etc. [½]
For those reasons that are sustainable in the long term, the insurer should consider changing its approach and, if appropriate, should implement new processes quickly. [½]
Product design: Consider reducing benefits, so that the insurer can offer more affordable products to customers who might otherwise not renew. [½]
Ideally, the reduction in the expected claims should be greater than the perceived reduction in value. [½]

Possible ways of reducing the cost of benefits include: excluding claims in respect of certain medical conditions or certain treatment, eg psychiatric treatment, restricting treatment only to certain hospitals, ... the insurer should also try to make arrangements for special discounts rates for treatment with those hospitals in return for encouraging (or forcing) their customers to use these hospitals. [1½]
limiting payments per treatment or numbers of treatment, eg up to six physiotherapy treatments per claim, limited to £25 per treatment. [½]
limiting aggregate payments per year, eg £10,000 pa. [½]
providing private cover only if State waiting lists are longer than, say, six weeks. [½]
imposing or increasing an excess per claim (or per annum). [½]
The drawback with restricting benefits is that it may be unpopular with customers who would expect complete peace of mind from their cover (ie full indemnity). [½]
A case of excluding treatment for a life-threatening condition may result in bad publicity for the insurer. [½]
The conditions to be excluded will therefore have to be chosen very carefully and should be available from the State as an alternative. [½]
All benefit restrictions should therefore be communicated very carefully and clearly to customers. [½]
The product could be designed to encourage greater use of State treatments, ... eg by offering generous payments if the policyholder is treated by the State rather than privately. [1]
Any changes made to the product should be designed to address the insurer's problems, and attempt to meet customers' needs as they change over time. [½]

An extreme solution would be to offer cash rather than indemnity benefits. These would provide a cash amount for certain types of treatment (eg £50 per consultation, £25 for each night's stay in hospital). [½]
Such products would be cheaper and this simple design may be more attractive to certain market segments. [½]
Persistency: Consider ways to improve persistency, eg encourage the use of payment by direct debit. [½]

A special team could be established with the aim of keeping in touch with customers, particularly just before renewal, to find out whether they are satisfied with their products, ... resolve any grievances and remind them of the importance of the cover they have. [1]
Pricing structures that limit (or remove) the effect of price increases would help customers to budget better and reduce the number of people leaving on account of price. [½]
For example, individual premium increases could be guaranteed not to increase by more than, say, 5% pa. [½]
However, guaranteeing premiums will significantly increase the risk to this insurer. Required margins on premiums may not be attractive to customers. [½]
Good quality customer service and contact with customers should be maintained throughout the policy lifetime to aid retention. [½]

Include (relatively cheap) benefits that the customer may use even when they do not need private treatment to give customers the feeling that they are getting something for their premiums, eg dedicated health information lines. [1]
Other actions: The insurer could / should:

undertake analysis to identify the profitability of each product, and the sources of the profit / loss, and take action as a result. ... eg reduce the proportion of business in less profitable sectors. [1]
review its selling methods to assess the success and contribution to profits of each distribution channel. [½]
new distribution channels should be considered. [½]
review claims handling procedures and underwriting standards to ensure that they are effective. [½]
review administration expenses to ensure that the subsidiary is being efficient, ... this should be balanced against the need to provide good customer service to improve persistency. [1]
analyse and identify the reasons for the increase in technical provisions - eg if this is due to errors or inappropriate processing these must be rectified. [½]

ascertain and allow for the expected impact of any expected changes in government, (eg which could affect State or PMI regulation) ... and amend its business plans as new information comes to light. [1]
review its investment strategy in the light of changing solvency position, increasing technical provisions, etc. [½]
amend the investment strategy if necessary, probably to reflect a more closely matched position, by mainly investing in short-term stable assets, such as cash on deposit. [½]
regularly monitor the effects of the actions implemented and adjust if necessary. [½]
